text: Hi everyone, this video demonstrates some tips and tricks for doing a low anterior resection in an obese patient. This patient has a rectal cancer, eleven centimetres from the anal verge. Doing a laparoscopic colorectal resection in an obese patient is challenging.
the oncological profile of the operation should be of prime importance. It's therefore vital that the vessel ligation and dissection of the TME plane should never be compromised.
The surgeon demonstrates the fat-laden appearance of the mesocolon and mesentery, applying traction on the pedicle of the inferior mesenteric artery to facilitate dissection.

The surgeon begins dissection at the sacral promontory, progressing upwards to the top of the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, aiming to identify the ureter and develop a plane beneath the inferior mesenteric artery and vein.

Oncology of this procedure should never be compromised although it's done laparoscopically. It's therefore important to do a high ligation and dissect the IMA as close to its origin from the aorta as possible.
The surgeon demonstrates the dissection and freeing of the top of the TME plane, keeping the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) taut to facilitate its separation from the overlying fatty tissue.

The surgeon attempts to skeletonize the inferior mesenteric artery, aiming to keep the lymph nodes and surrounding tissue on the resection side, though the presence of excessive fat in obese patients makes this challenging.

The surgeon dissects the inferior mesenteric artery up to its origin from the aorta, with the ureter clearly visible and preserved. A surgical plane is created between the inferior mesenteric artery and vein.
The surgeon observes the bulky pedicle of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), indicating a potential for a substantial lymph node harvest in the final specimen.

The surgeon uses an instrument to push toward the opposite side of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) and inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) pedicle, tautening the vascular pedicle and revealing the ureter from the opposite side in an obese patient.
The surgeon exposes the origin of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) from the aorta, highlighting the anatomical relationship between these structures.
In part two of this video we will demonstrate the dissection of the TME plane and a nerve preserving low anterior dissection. Many thanks for watching.
